title: Docker for ML Deployment Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of containerizing ML models for reproducible, production-ready deployment.

questions.question: In a Dockerfile, why should you COPY requirements.txt and RUN pip install BEFORE copying your application source code?

options.text: Because Docker requires files to be copied in alphabetical order
To leverage Docker's layer caching — the expensive pip install step is only re-run when requirements.txt changes, not on every code change
Source code can only be read after packages are installed
It reduces the final image size by compressing the packages

questions.question: What is the key benefit of a multi-stage Docker build for an ML image?

options.text: It trains multiple ML models simultaneously
It reduces the final runtime image size by discarding build-time tools and dependencies in the final stage
It allows the container to scale horizontally on its own
It speeds up model inference by 10x

questions.question: What is the risk of using `FROM python:3.11` with the `latest` tag instead of a specific tag like `python:3.11-slim`?

options.text: latest is not a valid Docker tag and will cause a build error
The base image can update without warning, breaking reproducibility and potentially changing installed system libraries
The image will not work on Linux servers
pip install will be slower on the latest tag

questions.question: How should you provide database passwords and API keys to a running Docker container?

options.text: Hardcode them into the Dockerfile using ENV instructions and commit to git
Write them inside the source code files
Inject them as environment variables at runtime using --env-file or a secrets manager like AWS Secrets Manager
Bake them into the model pickle file

questions.question: What does the HEALTHCHECK instruction in a Dockerfile do?

options.text: It validates the model's accuracy against a test dataset on startup
It defines a command Docker runs periodically to check if the container is healthy, used by orchestrators like Kubernetes to route traffic
It checks for security vulnerabilities in the installed packages
It pings the Docker Hub registry to check for image updates
